questions.text: Bright lines in the pattern show:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In the context of bright lines in a pattern, absorption refers to the process in which an atom or a molecule absorbs specific wavelengths of light when it transitions from a lower energy state to a higher energy state. When an external source of white light passes through a sample of gas or vapour, the atoms or molecules in the sample absorb light at certain discrete wavelengths. This absorption results in dark lines on the spectrum, known as absorption lines.

The correct option is b) "Emission." Bright lines in a pattern are primarily associated with the emission spectrum of an element or molecule. When an atom or molecule in an excited state returns to a lower energy state, it emits photons of specific wavelengths. These emitted photons form bright lines on the spectrum, which are characteristic of the element or molecule.
"Release" is not the correct term to describe the phenomenon of bright lines in a pattern. The process that gives rise to bright lines is emission, not release.

"Free particle" is not directly related to the observation of bright lines in a pattern. Bright lines in a spectrum arise from the emission of photons by atoms or molecules, not from the behaviour of free particles.

questions.explanation: A spectral line is a dark or bright line in an otherwise uniform and continuous spectrum, resulting from the emission or absorption of light of a particular frequency/wavelength. A bright line in the pattern shows the emission line spectra. It occurs when a hot gas emits radiation of a particular wavelength/frequency.

questions.text: Which source is associated with a line emission spectra?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is not associated with a line emission spectrum. Electric fibres typically refer to optical fibres used for transmitting light signals in telecommunications or other applications. They are not known for producing line emission spectra.

This option is associated with a line emission spectrum. Neon street signs and other similar neon lights work based on the phenomenon of line emission. When an electric current passes through a tube filled with neon gas, the gas atoms become excited and emit light at specific wavelengths, creating the characteristic bright colours seen in neon signs.
This option is not associated with a line emission spectrum. A red traffic light is typically produced using conventional incandescent or LED lights, which do not produce line emission spectra.

This option is not associated with a line emission spectrum. The sun produces a continuous spectrum rather than a line emission spectrum. A continuous spectrum contains a broad range of colours, whereas a line emission spectrum consists of specific discrete lines of colour at certain wavelengths.

questions.explanation: Neon lighting consists of brightly glowing, electrified glass tubes or bulbs that contain rarefied neon or other gases. Neon lights are a type of cold cathode gas-discharge light. Hence, neon street signs are associated with the line emission spectrum.

questions.text: X-rays are used to measure:

options.explanation: X-ray uses a very small dose of ionising radiation to produce pictures of any bone in the body. It is commonly used to diagnose fractured bones or joint dislocation.

A thermometer is an instrument that measures temperature. It can measure the temperature of a solid, such as food, a liquid such as water, or a gas such as air. The three most common units of measurement for temperature are Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin.
To measure blood pressure, a sphygmomanometer is used.
Only option A gives the correct answer; hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: X-ray uses a very small dose of ionising radiation to produce pictures of any bone in the body. It is commonly used to diagnose fractured bones or joint dislocation.

questions.text: The image received by the absorbed X-ray is _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The image obtained by X-ray imaging is both inverted and real. Although this option does mention one of the features, option C is the best choice since it mentions both features of the image.
The image obtained by X-ray imaging is both inverted and real. Although this option does mention one of the features, option C is the best choice since it mentions both features of the image.

X-rays are electromagnetic waves that can penetrate through materials and are used in various medical and industrial applications, including X-ray imaging. In X-ray imaging, X-rays are passed through the body or object being imaged, and the image is captured on a detector. The image obtained by X-ray imaging is both inverted and real. Inverted means that the image appears upside down compared to the actual object, while real means that the image is formed on the opposite side of the detector from the X-ray source, forming a real image.
The image obtained by X-ray imaging is both inverted and real. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: X-rays are electromagnetic waves that can penetrate through materials and are used in various medical and industrial applications, including X-ray imaging. In X-ray imaging, X-rays are passed through the body or object being imaged, and the image is captured on a detector. The image obtained by X-ray imaging is both inverted and real. Inverted means that the image appears upside down compared to the actual object, while real means that the image is formed on the opposite side of the detector from the X-ray source, forming a real image.

questions.text: For atomic spectra, atomic gas or vapor at a pressure that is much _ than atmospheric pressure is excited.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect as atomic spectra are obtained at low-pressure conditions, where the collision frequency is low, and the probability of radiative de-excitation is high.
This option is incorrect as atomic spectra are obtained at low-pressure conditions, where the collision frequency is low, and the probability of radiative de-excitation is high.

Atomic spectra are produced when the atoms of an element in a gaseous state are excited to a higher energy level, and then they emit radiation when they return to their original energy state. Suppose the pressure is increased to atmospheric pressure or above. In that case, the excited atoms will collide more frequently with other atoms or molecules in the gas, which can cause energy to be lost in the collision instead of being emitted as radiation. This collisional de-excitation process will produce a broad, continuous spectrum, which will obscure the atomic spectral lines. Therefore, atomic spectra are obtained at low-pressure conditions, where the collision frequency is low, and the probability of radiative de-excitation is high.
Option C is correct.

questions.explanation: Atomic spectra are produced when the atoms of an element in a gaseous state are excited to a higher energy level, and then they emit radiation when they return to their original energy state. If the pressure is increased to atmospheric pressure or above, the excited atoms will collide more frequently with other atoms or molecules in the gas, which can cause energy to be lost in the collision instead of being emitted as radiation. This collisional de-excitation process will produce a broad, continuous spectrum, which will obscure the atomic spectral lines. Therefore, atomic spectra are obtained at low-pressure conditions, where the collision frequency is low, and the probability of radiative de-excitation is high.

questions.text: Calculate the wavelength of photon associated with 3.1 x 1014 Hz frequency.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect according to the calculations.
This option is incorrect according to the calculations.

The wavelength of an electromagnetic wave can be calculated using the formula:wavelength = speed of light/frequencywhere speed of light = 3 x 108 m/sSubstituting the given frequency f = 3 x 1014 Hz, we get:wavelength = 3 x 108 / 3 x 1014wavelength = 1 x 10-6 mTherefore, the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave is 1 micrometre.
This option is incorrect according to the calculations.

questions.explanation: A photon’s speed is 3x108 m/s (as it is the fundamental particle of light, and this is light's speed), and since the velocity of a wave is equal to the product of its wavelength and frequency, speed can be divided by frequency (given in the question) to get wavelength, which is 1 micrometre here.The wavelength of an electromagnetic wave can be calculated using the formula:wavelength = speed of light/frequencywhere speed of light = 3 x 108 m/sSubstituting the given frequency f = 3 x 1014 Hz, we get:wavelength = 3 x 108 / 3 x 1014wavelength = 1 x 10-6 mTherefore, the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave is 1 micrometre.

questions.text: The Davisson Germer experiment is used to explain:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Interference: When two coherent waves (light, sound, water) meet, their displacements add up. The Davisson-Germer experiment is used to explain diffraction. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Polarisation: A property of transverse waves (like light, sound, radio) where oscillations occur in a specific plane, perpendicular to wave travel. The Davisson-Germer experiment is used to explain diffraction. Hence, this option is incorrect.

In the Davisson-Germer experiment, a beam of electrons is fired at a crystal of nickel. The electrons are diffracted by the crystal, producing a pattern of bright and dark spots on a detector screen. This pattern is similar to the interference pattern produced by light passing through a diffraction grating. The observed pattern can only be explained if the electrons behave like waves and interfere with each other as they pass through the crystal. The diffraction of electrons in the Davisson-Germer experiment provided strong evidence for the wave-like nature of matter and demonstrated that electrons can exhibit both wave-like and particle-like behaviour, depending on the experimental setup.
The Davisson-Germer experiment is used to explain diffraction. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: In the Davisson-Germer experiment, a beam of electrons is fired at a crystal of nickel. The electrons are diffracted by the crystal, producing a pattern of bright and dark spots on a detector screen. This pattern is similar to the interference pattern produced by light passing through a diffraction grating. The observed pattern can only be explained if the electrons behave like waves and interfere with each other as they pass through the crystal. The diffraction of electrons in the Davisson-Germer experiment provided strong evidence for the wave-like nature of matter and demonstrated that electrons can exhibit both wave-like and particle-like behaviour, depending on the experimental setup.

questions.text: Why are x-rays used in crystallography?
questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Optics, Nature of Light and Optical Instruments

options.explanation: When we use X-rays in crystallography, they interfere and give us valuable information about the arrangement of atoms in the crystal lattice. Hence, this statement is wrong that we use x-rays to prevent interference.
During crystallography, X-rays do not prevent diffraction, instead, they are used because the diffract. Hence, this option is incorrect.
During crystallography, X-rays are not used specifically to perform interference. Interference is just a natural phenomenon that occurs during crystallography. Hence, this option is incorrect.

We use X-rays in crystallography because the atoms in crystals have very small spacings. X-rays are the only electromagnetic radiations that have a small enough wavelength to study crystals. When x-rays interact with crystals, they diffract and this is why they are specifically used. Hence, this option is the correct option.

questions.explanation: We use X-rays in crystallography because the atoms in crystals have very small spacings. X-rays are the only electromagnetic radiations that have a small enough wavelength to study crystals. When x-rays interact with crystals, they diffract and this is why they are specifically used. questions.text: Which rays cannot be produced by electronic transitions?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Electron transition within the atom releases electromagnetic radiation (chargeless). Alpha rays have a charge. Hence we can safely say that electron transitions within an atom wouldn't cause the release of alpha rays.

Infrared radiation is produced when molecules undergo electronic transitions. Infrared radiation has longer wavelengths than visible light, which means that it has lower energy. Infrared radiation is used in a variety of applications, such as remote sensing, thermal imaging, and spectroscopy.

Gamma rays are not produced by electronic transitions. Gamma rays are a type of electromagnetic radiation that has the highest energy and shortest wavelength in the electromagnetic spectrum. They are produced by nuclear reactions, such as radioactive decay or nuclear fusion. Gamma rays can be dangerous to living organisms because they can penetrate through matter and cause damage to cells and DNA.

options.text: All of these options are correct.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as not all the listed rays meet the criteria of being unrelated to electronic transitions. Only alpha rays are not produced by electronic transitions.

questions.explanation: Alpha particles are a type of nuclear radiation and consist of two protons and two neutrons, originating from the nucleus, not from electronic transitions. Therefore, they cannot be produced by electronic transitions, which involve changes in the energy levels of electrons in an atom. Infrared radiation is electromagnetic and results from electronic transitions. Although gamma rays are also electromagnetic, they are generally associated with nuclear processes rather than electronic transitions. Thus, the correct answer is that alpha rays cannot be produced by electronic transitions.

questions.text: The lifetime of an excited state is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Option A suggests that the lifetime of an excited state is 10−6 seconds, which is one microsecond. This duration is relatively long for atomic or molecular excited states. Excited states typically have much shorter lifetimes, often on the order of nanoseconds or even picoseconds. Therefore, option A is unlikely to be correct.

Option B suggests that the lifetime of an excited state is 10−7 seconds. This duration is relatively long for atomic or molecular excited states. Excited states typically have much shorter lifetimes, often on the order of nanoseconds or even picoseconds. Therefore, option B is unlikely to be correct.

Option C suggests that the lifetime of an excited state is 10−8 seconds, which is one hundred nanoseconds. This duration is plausible for the lifetime of an excited state, as it aligns with typical lifetimes observed in atomic and molecular systems. Many excited states decay rapidly within nanoseconds, making option C a reasonable choice.

Option D suggests that the lifetime of an excited state is 10−3 seconds, which is one millisecond. This duration is significantly longer than typical excited state lifetimes observed in atomic and molecular systems. Excited states with lifetimes on the order of milliseconds are uncommon, especially in the absence of special conditions. Therefore, option D is unlikely to be correct.

questions.explanation: The lifetime of atoms in an excited state refers to the time duration during which the electrons of the atoms will remain in their excited state. This is normally 10-8 seconds. Many excited states decay rapidly within nanoseconds, making option C a reasonable choice.

questions.text: Why X-rays are used in crystallography?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Crystallography is not used to prevent interference. Crystallography is a scientific technique used to study the arrangement of atoms in crystals. It involves directing X-rays at a crystal and analyzing the diffraction pattern produced by the X-rays to determine the positions of the atoms in the crystal lattice. Interference, on the other hand, is a phenomenon that occurs when two or more waves meet and combine to form a resultant wave. Interference can be constructive, where the waves reinforce each other, or destructive, where the waves cancel each other out. Interference can be observed in many different contexts, such as in light waves, sound waves, and water waves. However, it is not directly related to crystallography.

Crystallography is not used to prevent diffraction. Crystallography relies on diffraction to study the arrangement of atoms in crystals. X-ray crystallography is a technique used to determine the structure of crystals by analyzing the diffraction pattern produced when X-rays are scattered by the atoms in the crystal lattice.

Crystallography is not used to perform interference. Crystallography is a technique used to study the arrangement of atoms in crystals by analyzing the diffraction pattern produced when X-rays are scattered by the atoms in the crystal lattice. Interference, on the other hand, is a phenomenon that occurs when two or more waves meet and combine to form a resultant wave.

Crystallography is the study of the arrangement of atoms in crystals. X-ray crystallography is a technique used to determine the structure of crystals by analyzing the diffraction pattern produced when X-rays are scattered by the atoms in the crystal lattice. When X-rays are directed at a crystal, they are diffracted by the atoms in the crystal lattice, producing a diffraction pattern that can be used to determine the positions of the atoms in the crystal. The spacing between the atoms in the crystal lattice is on the order of the wavelength of x-rays, making x-rays ideal for this application. The diffraction pattern produced by X-ray crystallography contains information about the positions of the atoms in the crystal, as well as their arrangement and orientation. This information can be used to determine the structure of the crystal, including the positions of the atoms and the bonds between them. X-ray crystallography is widely used in chemistry, biology, and materials science to study the structure of molecules, proteins, and other materials.

questions.explanation: Crystallography is the study of the arrangement of atoms in crystals. X-ray crystallography is a technique used to determine the structure of crystals by analyzing the diffraction pattern produced when X-rays are scattered by the atoms in the crystal lattice. When X-rays are directed at a crystal, they are diffracted by the atoms in the crystal lattice, producing a diffraction pattern that can be used to determine the positions of the atoms in the crystal. The spacing between the atoms in the crystal lattice is on the order of the wavelength of x-rays, making x-rays ideal for this application. The diffraction pattern produced by X-ray crystallography contains information about the positions of the atoms in the crystal, as well as their arrangement and orientation. This information can be used to determine the structure of the crystal, including the positions of the atoms and the bonds between them. X-ray crystallography is widely used in chemistry, biology, and materials science to study the structure of molecules, proteins, and other materials.

questions.text: Laser light is a type of _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Spontaneous emission refers to the process where an atom or molecule in an excited state releases its energy in the form of electromagnetic radiation (light) without any external influence. In this process, the emission of photons occurs randomly, and the emitted light is not coherent or directional. Spontaneous emission is common in many natural light sources, such as the sun or an incandescent light bulb, where photons are emitted in various directions and phases.

Laser light is a type of stimulated emission where one atom is stimulated which further stimulates other atoms to release electromagnetic radiations.Stimulated emission is a fundamental process in the operation of a laser. In stimulated emission, an incoming photon interacts with an excited atom or molecule, causing it to release its energy in the form of another photon that is identical in terms of energy, frequency, phase, and direction to the incoming photon. This process leads to the amplification and coherent nature of laser light. Hence, Option B is correct.

options.text: Both Options A and B are correct
options.explanation: Only B is correct hence it is an incorrect option.

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: As B is correct, it is an incorrect option.

questions.explanation: Laser light is a type of stimulated emission where one atom is stimulated which further stimulates other atoms to release electromagnetic radiations. Hence, Option B is correct.

questions.text: The formula for the Paschen series for the Hydrogen spectrum is _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This formula does not represent the Paschen series. It represents the Balmer series, which involves transitions to the n=2 level. The range of n values (3, 4, 5) does not apply to the Paschen series.

This formula does not represent the Paschen series. It is incorrect because it mistakenly uses n=4 as the lower energy level, which is not characteristic of any well-known series in the hydrogen spectrum.
This formula is incorrect for the Paschen series. It implies transitions ending at n=4, which corresponds to the Brackett series, not the Paschen series.

This formula accurately represents the Paschen series. It involves transitions from higher energy levels to n=3, which is characteristic of the Paschen series in the infrared region of the hydrogen spectrum.

questions.explanation: The correct formula for the Paschen series is Option D: 1/λ=Rh(1/32 - 1/n2); n=4,5,6. The Paschen series refers to electron transitions ending at the n=3 energy level, resulting in spectral lines in the infrared region. The other options represent different series or incorrect configurations: Option A represents the Balmer series, transitioning to n=2. Option B does not correspond to any recognised series due to its incorrect configuration. Option C represents the Brackett series, transitioning to n=4.

questions.text: For the first time in 1914, Henry Moseley used X-rays for the determination of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In 1914, he observed and measured the X-ray spectra of various chemical elements using diffraction in crystals. Through this, he discovered a systematic relation between wavelength and atomic number. This discovery is now known as Moseley's Law. He found that certain lines in the X-ray spectrum of elements move by the same amount each time when we increase the atomic number by one.

Atomic mass is the mass of an atom, typically measured in atomic mass units (amu). While X-ray spectroscopy can provide valuable information about the electronic structure and atomic properties of elements, it is not primarily used for determining atomic mass. Atomic mass is usually determined through techniques such as mass spectrometry or by analysing the isotopic composition of elements.

Molecular mass refers to the mass of a molecule, which is the sum of the masses of the atoms in the molecule. X-ray spectroscopy is not typically used for determining molecular mass. Instead, molecular mass is often calculated based on the atomic masses of the constituent atoms in a molecule.

Equivalent mass is a concept primarily used in the context of chemical reactions and stoichiometry, representing the mass of a substance that reacts with or is equivalent to one mole of hydrogen ions (H⁺) in a chemical reaction. X-ray spectroscopy is not directly related to determining equivalent mass. Equivalent mass is typically calculated based on the stoichiometry of a reaction and the molar masses of the substances involved.

questions.explanation: In 1914, he observed and measured the X-ray spectra of various chemical elements using diffraction in crystals. Through this, he discovered a systematic relation between wavelength and atomic number. This discovery is now known as Moseley's Law. He found that certain lines in the X-ray spectrum of elements move by the same amount each time when we increase the atomic number by one.

questions.text: X-rays are also known as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Rydberg is a term associated with atomic physics, particularly the Rydberg formula used to calculate the wavelengths of spectral lines. 'Rydberg rays' is not a term used to refer to X-rays.

X-rays are also known as Roentgen rays, named after Wilhelm Conrad Roentgen, who discovered them in 1895. He found that these rays could penetrate substances and produce images, revolutionising medical imaging and diagnostics.
Ultraviolet (UV) rays have shorter wavelengths than visible light but longer than X-rays. While UV rays are used in various applications, they are not another name for X-rays.
'Zig-zag rays' is not a recognised term in the context of X-rays or any standard scientific field.
'Ruby rays' is not a scientific term related to X-rays. The term 'ruby' usually refers to the gemstone, which does not emit X-rays.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Roentgen rays. X-rays were discovered by Wilhelm Conrad Roentgen in 1895, and in honour of his discovery, they are often called Roentgen rays. This discovery was pivotal in the development of diagnostic imaging in medicine. The other options are incorrect as they do not relate to the historical or scientific context of X-rays. Rydberg is associated with atomic spectra, ultraviolet rays are a different part of the electromagnetic spectrum, and 'zig-zag rays' and 'ruby rays' are not scientifically recognised terms related to X-rays.

questions.text: The atomic spectra deals with the measurement of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The atomic spectra involve measurements of the wavelengths of electromagnetic radiation emitted or absorbed during electron transitions between energy levels.

options.text: Intensities of electromagnetic radiations emitted by atoms
options.explanation: Atomic spectra also involve measuring the intensities of radiation emitted as electrons move to lower energy levels.

options.text: Intensities of electromagnetic radiations absorbed by atoms
options.explanation: Measurements of the intensities of radiation absorbed during electron transitions to higher energy levels are part of atomic spectra studies.
All these aspects—wavelengths and intensities of emitted and absorbed radiation—are fundamental components of atomic spectral measurements.
This option is incomplete as it ignores the measurement of wavelengths, which is a key aspect of atomic spectra.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D: All of these options. Atomic spectra encompass the study of both the wavelengths and the intensities of electromagnetic radiation emitted and absorbed by atoms. When electrons transition between energy levels, they emit or absorb photons, whose characteristics—like wavelength and intensity—can be measured. Options A, B, and C all address partial aspects of these measurements, but the full scope of atomic spectra includes all these components.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is inconsistent with Bohr's set of postulates regarding the hydrogen atom model regarding the emission and absorption of light?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Energy levels of the electrons are stable and discrete
options.explanation: Energy levels of the electrons are stable and discrete: This statement is consistent with Bohr's model, as it describes how electrons occupy quantised energy levels around the nucleus.

options.text: An electron emits or absorbs radiation only when making a transition from one energy level to another
options.explanation: An electron emits or absorbs radiation only when making a transition from one energy level to another: This statement is also consistent with Bohr's model, as it describes the conditions under which electrons emit or absorb radiation.

options.text: To jump from lower energy to higher energy, an electron must absorb a photon of precisely the right frequency such that the photon's energy equals the energy difference between the two orbits
options.explanation: To jump from a lower energy to a higher energy, an electron must absorb a photon of precisely the right frequency such that the photon's energy equals the energy difference between the two orbits. This statement is consistent with Bohr's model and describes the process of absorption of photons by electrons to move to higher energy levels.

options.text: To jump from a higher energy to a lower energy, an electron absorbs a photon of frequency such that the photon's energy is exactly the energy difference between the two orbits
options.explanation: Bohr’s Model has quantised angular momentum, which is option A. It is consistent with the model. Energy changes only take place if an electron changes shells, as stated in option B. It is consistent with the model. When an electron jumps from a lower orbit to a higher orbit, it absorbs energy such that ΔE= E2-E1. It is consistent with the model. When an electron jumps from a higher orbit to a lower orbit, it emits energy. In the question statement, it's wrong that “It absorbs energy”. Hence, this is not consistent with the model. Thus, Option D is correct, while others are rendered as incorrect.
This option is incorrect since the postulate in option D is indeed inconsistent, making D the correct choice.

questions.explanation: Bohr’s Model has quantised angular momentum, which is option A. It is consistent with the model. Energy changes only take place if an electron changes shells, as stated in option B. It is consistent with the model. When an electron jumps from a lower orbit to a higher orbit, it absorbs energy such that ΔE= E2-E1. It is consistent with the model. When an electron jumps from a higher orbit to a lower orbit, it emits energy. In the question statement, it's wrong that “It absorbs energy”. Hence, this is not consistent with the model. Thus, Option D is correct, while others are rendered as incorrect.

The correct answer. For the shortest wavelength in the Balmer series, the transition is from n = ∞ to n = 2. Using the Rydberg formula, 1/λ = R(1/22 - 1/∞2), we find λ = 364.6 nm.

This option provides a wavelength longer than the correct shortest wavelength in the Balmer series, which is 364.6 nm.

questions.text: Which of the following X-rays has the largest intensity?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Kα X-rays are produced when an electron transitions from the second energy level (n=2) to the first energy level (n=1) in an atom, resulting in a significant release of energy. This transition is responsible for their high intensity and ionization capability. Kα X-rays have the highest intensity among X-ray emissions due to their effective ionization of atoms over short distances, permitting them to cause substantial effects when interacting with matter.

Kß X-rays are generated when an electron falls from the third energy level (n=3) to the first energy level (n=1). While they have a higher penetration capability than Kα, their ionization power is lower, leading to lesser intensity compared to Kα X-rays.

Kγ X-rays are produced by transitions from higher energy levels, typically resulting in lower intensity compared to Kα emissions. Although Kγ X-rays have high penetration power, they are less effective in ionizing atoms compared to Kα X-rays, making them less intense overall.

options.text: All of these options have the same intensity
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because each type of X-ray has different ionization and intensity characteristics. Kα X-rays are known to have the highest intensity among the options provided.

questions.explanation: Kα X-rays exhibit the largest intensity due to the considerable energy released when an electron transitions from the n=2 to n=1 energy level in an atom. This transition generates a strong ionization effect, allowing Kα X-rays to ionize a greater number of atoms in a short distance, resulting in their higher intensity. In contrast, Kß X-rays, while having greater penetration power, possess lower ionization and intensity. Kγ X-rays, although they penetrate effectively, also do not have the same ionization capability, making them less intense than Kα X-rays. The fourth option incorrectly assumes equal intensity across all types, which is not the case.

questions.text: Laser beam can be used to generate three-dimensional image of object in a process called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because "computed technology" is not a recognized term or process in the context of generating three-dimensional images using laser beams.
This option is incorrect because computed tomography (CT) involves X-rays and not laser beams for creating cross-sectional images of objects.

Holography is a technique based on wave interference to record and form three-dimensional images. Lasers offer a practical way to produce sharp holographic images because of their monochromatic and coherent light for pronounced interference patterns.
This option is incorrect because computerized axial tomography (CAT) also refers to the same imaging technique as computed tomography (CT), which uses X-rays and not laser beams.

questions.explanation: Holography is a technique based on wave interference to record and form three-dimensional images. Lasers offer a practical way to produce sharp holographic images because of their monochromatic and coherent light for pronounced interference patterns.

questions.text: The characteristic X-ray spectrum is due to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The absorption of neutrons by target material
options.explanation: Neutrons do not directly produce characteristic X-rays. They can induce nuclear reactions, but these typically result in gamma rays, not characteristic X-rays.

options.text: The bombardment of target material by protons
options.explanation: Protons can cause X-ray production through Bremsstrahlung radiation, but characteristic X-rays result from electron transitions, not proton interactions.

options.text: The bombardment of target material by electrons
options.explanation: The characteristic x-rays are obtained due to the transition of electron from inner orbits of the atoms, so the target is bombarded by electrons.

options.text: The bombardment of target material by alpha particles
options.explanation: Alpha particles can cause ionization and Bremsstrahlung radiation, but characteristic X-rays involve specific electron energy transitions, not interactions with alpha particles.

questions.explanation: The characteristic x-rays are obtained due to the transition of electron from inner orbits of the atoms, so the target is bombarded by electrons.

questions.text: The units of planks constant are the same as that of :
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The unit of planck's constant is equivalent to that of angular momentum. Their dimensional formulas are the same but both physical quantities are not the same. Planck's constant gives the relation between energy and frequency.Planck's constant (represented by the symbol h) has the units of joule-seconds (J.s) in the International System of Units (SI). The quantity of angular momentum also has the units of kilogram-metre squared per second (kg.m^2/s), which can be expressed in terms of J.s since joules and kilogram-metres squared are equivalent units of energy and momentum, respectively

On the other hand, work has the units of joules (J), force has the units of newtons (N), and torque has the units of newton-metres (N.m). Therefore, the units of Planck's constant are not the same as those of work, force, or torque.

On the other hand, work has the units of joules (J), force has the units of newtons (N), and torque has the units of newton-metres (N.m). Therefore, the units of Planck's constant are not the same as those of work, force, or torque.

On the other hand, work has the units of joules (J), force has the units of newtons (N), and torque has the units of newton-metres (N.m). Therefore, the units of Planck's constant are not the same as those of work, force, or torque.

questions.explanation: The unit of planck's constant is equivalent to that of angular momentum. Their dimensional formulas are the same but both physical quantities are not the same. Planck's constant gives the relation between energy and frequency

Paschen series lies in infra-red region of spectrum of hydrogen atom.
Brackett series lies in the infra-red region,more precisely in the far infra-red region of hydrogen spectrum.
Pfund series lies in the infra-red region,more precisely in the far infra-red region of hydrogen spectrum.

questions.explanation: Lyman series of hydrogen atom lies in ultraviolet region, Balmer series lies in visible region while Pfund and Paschen series lie in infrared region.

questions.text: Fluorescence is the property of absorbing radiant energy of …... frequency and re-emitting energy of _ frequency in the visible region of electromagnetic spectrum.
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: In fluorescence, a photon is absorbed which takes an atom or molecule to a higher energy level. To conserve momentum, this higher level of energy might consist of a combination of electronic transition, rotational transition, vibrational transition, and kinetic energy. The atom or molecule may remain in a higher energy state for a long enough period of time to collide with other atoms or molecules and transfer some of the energy to them. When a photon of lower energy is emitted, the remaining energy may also be emitted as a photon, but it is unlikely that the energy and momentum can both be conserved, so much of the time the energy remains in the molecule until a collision happens to exchange the energy by means of a collision. Eventually, a low-energy photon is emitted, but by then some of the energy has become part of the internal energy of the material, raising its temperature ever so slightly. So, some energy is released as a second photon and some of the energy contributes to heating up the fluorescent material.

questions.text: The wavelength associated with an electron is of the order of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Visible light ranges from 400 nm (violet) to 700 nm (red), still much larger than electron wavelengths.

For an electron moving at high speeds (e.g., in an atom or a particle accelerator), its wavelength typically falls in the X-ray region of the electromagnetic spectrum, which is around 0.01 nm to 10 nm.
Radio waves have even longer wavelengths (1 mm to 100 km), far exceeding electron wavelengths.
Infrared wavelengths range from ~700 nm to 1 mm, which are much longer than the wavelengths typically associated with electrons.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is X-rays. Electrons, when moving at high speeds, such as in a hydrogen atom or in a particle accelerator, have wavelengths that fall within the X-ray region of the electromagnetic spectrum. This range is approximately 0.01 nm to 10 nm, which is much shorter than visible light, infrared, or radio waves. Visible light ranges from 400 nm to 700 nm, infrared from ~700 nm to 1 mm, and radio waves from 1 mm to 100 km, all of which are much longer than electron wavelengths.

questions.text: For gaining atomic spectra, an evacuated glass tube is filled with:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Neon can also produce spectral lines, but it is not typically used to obtain atomic spectra.

Atomic spectra are the spectra of frequencies of electromagnetic waves emitted or absorbed during transitions of electrons between energy levels within an atom. It is different for every atom. Hydrogen gas is most commonly used to fill the glass tube as it results in different series, namely the Lyman, Balmer, Paschen, Brackett and Pfund series.
Carbon dioxide can also produce spectral lines, but it is not typically used to produce atomic spectra.
Sulphur dioxide can also produce spectral lines, but it is not typically used to produce atomic spectra.

questions.explanation: Atomic spectra are the spectra of frequencies of electromagnetic waves emitted or absorbed during transitions of electrons between energy levels within an atom. It is different for every atom. Hydrogen gas is most commonly used to fill the glass tube as it results in different series, namely the Lyman, Balmer, Paschen, Brackett and Pfund series.
